text:
Anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization is a burgeoning catalytic transformation that holds significant promise in petroleum-derived petroleum-based feedstocks and renewable resources. Despite its emergent yet considerable importance, the structural pathway(s) underlying transition metal-free photoredox catalytic processes has not yet been fully clarified and remains subject to some debate. Herein, we report unequivocal insight into the structural catalytic pathways underlying the transition metal-free anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization catalyzed by the photocatalyst 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr⁺). Notably, the alkene cation radical species can be clearly observed by means of transient absorption spectroscopy, which strongly supports the single-electron transfer pathway over that of the previously-accepted exciplex mechanism. Laser flash photolysis investigations into Mes-Acr⁺-induced signal-generating photochemistry revealed that the phenylthiyl radical could rapidly oxidize the persistently-existing Mes-Acr• species with a second-order rate constant of $3.1 \times 10^9 \text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$, and accordingly the rapid oxidation between the Mes-Acr• species is regarded as the coupling catalyst cycle between photoredox and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT).

In situ generated 2-phenylmalononitrile (PMN) functions as the H-atom acceptor that enables protonation of Mes-Acr⁻. We also demonstrate that diphenyl disulfide ((PhS)2) can act as a HAT cocatalyst. Upon photolysis, (PhS)2 can be cleaved into (PhS)·, which shares the same reactive manifold as thiophenol (PhSH). Our DFT calculations confirmed that PhSH is indeed a more efficient H-atom donor than PMN due to lower energy barriers attributed to lesser structural distortions in the transition states. Mes-Acr⁺ also engages in reversible adduct binding in the off-cycle in the early stages of the reaction that we believe is initiated by thiolate binding PhS⁻. Analysis of the kinetic data indicated that the adduct binding and proton transfer steps possess considerable rate determining character to the catalytic reaction.
